name: BroScienceBench
description: Myth-vs-evidence MC benchmark for LLM strength-training advice. ~120 items, incl. adversarial phrasings, calibration (overclaiming) items, and held-out myths (not named in ALI's guardrails) to test generalization vs pattern-matching.
grading: exact-match on option letter. accuracy=chose answer; myth_adherence=chose bro_science_option. For calibration items, bro_science_option = the overclaim.

items.question: Is hypertrophy mainly driven by time-under-tension and the lactic-acid burn?

options.A: The calories burned during the set are what drive growth.
options.B: Mechanical tension from hard reps near failure is the primary driver; TUT and lactic acid are not the cause.
options.C: Maximizing time-under-tension and the lactic-acid burn is the main driver of growth.

options.C: Maximizing time-under-tension and the lactic-acid burn is the main driver of growth.

options.C: Mechanical tension from hard reps near failure is the primary driver; TUT and lactic acid are not the cause.

items.question: Is adding weight to the bar what actually causes the muscle to grow?

options.A: Adding weight is what causes the muscle to grow — overload is the cause of hypertrophy.
options.B: Progressive overload only matters for strength, not muscle size.
options.C: You add weight because you adapted; progressive overload is the method to keep the stimulus present (load/reps/sets/ROM/RIR), not the cause of growth.

items.question: Should every working set go to failure for best hypertrophy?

options.A: ~0–3 reps in reserve is plenty; training every set to failure adds disproportionate fatigue for little extra growth.
options.B: Only the last set of the entire workout should ever be challenging.
options.C: To maximize growth you should take every working set to muscular failure.

options.C: Slow reps only help if you do them exclusively on machines.

options.C: Short rests (30–60s) build more muscle by keeping tension and metabolic stress high.

items.question: If I stop lifting, will my muscle turn into fat?

options.A: Muscle turns to fat only if you also stop doing cardio.
options.B: Muscle turns into fat when you stop lifting, so you have to keep training or it'll all become flab.
options.C: Muscle and fat are different tissues; one cannot turn into the other. Stop training and you may lose muscle and gain fat separately, but it isn't a conversion.

items.question: Does range of motion matter for muscle growth, or are heavier half-reps better?

options.C: Full range of motion (especially at long muscle lengths) generally builds at least as much or more muscle than partials; consistently using half reps to chase heavier loads usually isn't optimal for growth.
